A large language model (LLM) is a language model trained with self-supervised machine learning on a vast amount of text, designed for natural language processing tasks, especially language generation. The largest and most capable LLMs are generative pre-trained transformers (GPTs) and provide the core capabilities of modern chatbots. LLMs can be fine-tuned for specific tasks or guided by prompt engineering. These models acquire predictive power regarding syntax, semantics, and ontologies inherent in human language corpora, but they also inherit inaccuracies and biases present in the data they

question: How do large language models like GPT-3 become capable of text generation?
context:
== Background ==
After the release of large language models such as GPT-3, a focus of research was up-scaling models, which in some instances showed major increases in emergent capabilities. The release of ChatGPT and its surprise success caused an increase in attention to large language models.
Compared with other responses to ChatGPT, Meta's Chief AI scientist Yann LeCun stated that large language models are best for aiding with writing.


== Versions ==

A generative pre-trained transformer (GPT) is a type of large language model (LLM) that is widely used in generative AI chatbots. GPTs are based on a deep learning architecture called the transformer. They are pre-trained on large datasets of unlabeled content, and able to generate novel content.

GPTs are primarily used to generate text, but can be trained to generate other kinds of data. For example, GPT-4o can process and generate text, images and audio. To improve performance on complex tasks, some GPTs, such as OpenAI o3, allocate more computation time analyzing the problem before generating an output, and are called reasoning models. In 2025, GPT-5 was released with a router that automatically selects whether to use a faster model or slower reasoning model based on task.

response: Large language models like GPT-3 become capable of text generation through a process called pre-training on large datasets of unlabeled content. This involves training the model on a deep learning architecture called the transformer, which is based on self-attention mechanisms. During pre-training, the model learns to predict missing words in a sentence, a process known as masked language modeling. After pre-training, the model can be fine-tuned on specific tasks by adjusting its weights to better perform on those tasks. This fine-tuning process often involves providing the model with additional task-specific data and optimizing its performance on that data. Some GPTs, such as OpenAI's reasoning models, also allocate more computation time for analyzing the problem before generating an output.

response: Some architectures used in building artificial neural networks for Large Language Models (LLMs) include Transformer-based models such as LLaMA, Mistral, and Falcon. These models learn to predict the next word on large-scale web corpora. Other examples include Neural Turing Machines (NTMs) (Santos et al., 2018) and Memory Networks (Weston et al., 2014). NTMs incorporate external memory to store and access information during computation, while Memory Networks use multiple memory components to encode input data and perform reasoning tasks. The specific implementation details, such as the number of layers, hidden units, and attention mechanisms, may vary between different models.

Abstract
We introduce Mistral 7B, a 7–billion-parameter language model engineered for
superior performance and efficiency. Mistral 7B outperforms the best open 13B
model (Llama 2) across all evaluated benchmarks, and the best released 34B

Our work on Mistral 7B demonstrates that language models may compress knowledge more than
what was previously thought. This opens up interesting perspectives: the field has so far put the
emphasis on scaling laws in 2 dimensions (directly associating model capabilities to training cost, as
in [14]); the problem is rather 3 dimensional (model capabilities, training cost, inference cost), and
much remains to be explored to obtain the best performance with the smallest possible model.

response: Large language models may not be considered plausible cognitive models due to their lack of understanding of the world beyond the data they were trained on. They do not possess the ability to reason about new concepts or generalize knowledge outside their training data. Furthermore, they do not truly learn from experience but rather memorize patterns in the data. This is because they are trained using unsupervised learning methods such as self-supervised learning or pretraining on large corpora without explicit labels. The absence of human-provided feedback during training also contributes to their inability to understand the real-world implications of their responses.

response: The purpose of the Constitution in training AI systems, as proposed in the paper "Constitutional AI: Harmlessness from AI Feedback" (McKinnon et al., 2022), is to ensure the AI system adheres to certain principles and preferences during its learning process. This is achieved through the Constitutional DPO algorithm, which optimizes the AI system's learning towards a set of preference data consisting of samples from the optimal policy and synthesized negative examples with AI feedback. The algorithm combines elements from various previous works such as Constitutional AI, RLCD, and DPO, aiming to collaborate with risk-aware humans and encourage the AI system to learn from principled negative examples. response: The ability of a language model to analyze images can significantly expand its range of applications by enabling it to perform tasks such as image-to-text generation and image classification. This is achieved through in-context learning, where the model is trained to learn from a small number of demonstration examples before making predictions on new, unseen data. In the case of image classification, this involves providing the model with a few pairs of demonstration images along with their corresponding labels, followed by a test image for which the model predicts the label. This approach, known as Retrieval-Augmented Multimodal Language Modeling, has been shown to outperform existing models while requiring less training data, compute, and parameters (as demonstrated in Figure 8). By incorporating knowledge from external sources through retrieval augmentation, these models can make more informed predictions and generate outputs that are more faithful to the retrieved evidence documents.

Evaluating the harms of language models.
A goal of modifying the behavior of language models
is to mitigate the harms of these models when they’re deployed in the real world. These risks have
been extensively documented (Bender et al., 2021; Bommasani et al., 2021; Kenton et al., 2021;
Weidinger et al., 2021; Tamkin et al., 2021). Language models can produce biased outputs (Dhamala
et al., 2021; Liang et al., 2021; Manela et al., 2021; Caliskan et al., 2017; Kirk et al., 2021), leak

model text from under-represented groups, due to prejudicial correlations in the training data (Xu
et al., 2021).
Modifying the behavior of language models to mitigate harms.
There are many ways to change
the generation behavior of language models. Solaiman and Dennison (2021) ﬁne-tune LMs on a
small, value-targeted dataset, which improves the models’ ability to adhere to these values on a
question answering task. Ngo et al. (2021) ﬁlter the pretraining dataset by removing documents on
which a language model has a high conditional likelihood of generating a set of researcher-written

response: Traditional position encoding methods in the architecture of pre-trained language models (PLMs) often suffer from a lack of thorough explanations on why they converge faster than other position encoding strategies, and there is no faithful explanation for their favorable property of long-term decay for internal token products, despite showing superior performance on long texts. To address these issues, the paper proposes a novel method called Rotary Position Embedding (RoPE). This method encodes relative position by multiplying the context representations with a rotation matrix, providing a clear theoretical interpretation. The RoPE method leverages positional information into the learning process of PLMs, offering a different approach compared to methods that add position encoding to the context representations through either a pre-defined function or a trainable absolute position encoding.

response: The Rotary Position Embedding (RoPE) approach in Transformers differs from the traditional additive method of position embedding in that it encodes position information multiplicatively rather than additively. Instead of adding position information to the context representation, RoPE encodes the absolute position with a rotation matrix and explicitly incorporates relative position dependency in the self-attention formulation. This is achieved by multiplying the context representations with a rotation matrix, which provides a clear theoretical interpretation. Unlike the additive method, where position information is altered in the expanded formulation of additive position encoding when applied with self-attention, RoPE naturally incorporates relative position information through rotation matrix product. However, due to the sparsity of the rotation matrix, a more computationally efficient realization is needed, which is discussed in the context.

Another method is presented by Solaiman and Dennison (2021), who fine-tune Language Models (LMs) on a small, value-targeted dataset, improving the models’ ability to adhere to specific values on a question answering task. Lastly, Ngo et al. (2021) filter the pretraining dataset by removing documents on which a language model has a high conditional likelihood of generating a set of researcher-written harmful content, thereby modifying the behavior of language models to mitigate potential harms.

response: The uniqueness of a reparameterized reward function within an equivalence class does not necessarily impact the selection of optimal policies in constrained reinforcement learning problems. According to Proposition 1, every equivalence class of reward functions has a unique reward function that can be reparameterized as r(x, y) = β log π(y|x) / πref(y|x) for some model π(y|x). This unique reward function within an equivalence class induces the same optimal policy under the constraint RL problem, as shown in Lemma 2 Restated. However, the human value distribution, which represents the utility of humans, may still be affected by input-specific changes in the reward function, even though the optimal policy remains the same.

response: To improve the performance of an AI model on various datasets, two primary approaches can be employed: dataset scaling and retrieval augmentation. Dataset scaling involves collecting larger datasets with a high focus on dataset quality to ensure proper train-test set overlap in language modeling. Retrieval augmentation, on the other hand, enhances model performance by integrating a retrieval component that selects relevant information from a corpus during generation. The effectiveness of these methods varies depending on the dataset's quality and similarity to the finetuning data. While dataset scaling may not always yield benefits if the data is not high-quality, retrieval augmentation consistently improves performance across different scales without exhibiting diminishing returns in the 125M–2.7B range studied. However, it's essential to verify the effectiveness of these techniques before applying them to special-domain datasets.

response: Recent research findings suggest several strategies for addressing the ethical and social risks associated with the deployment of language models. One approach involves detoxifying language models by removing or modifying harmful content from their training data (Welbl et al., 2021). Another strategy is the development of values-targeted datasets to adapt language models to society (Solaiman & Dennison, 2021). Furthermore, learning from human feedback can help language models summarize information more accurately and reduce the risk of producing biased outputs (Stiennon et al., 2020). However, the context does not provide specific implementation details or methodologies for these strategies. response: The criteria used to select labelers to effectively detect and rate sensitive content included agreement on sensitive speech flagging, agreement on rankings of model completions, and a sensitive demonstration writing test. Labelers were selected based on their performance in these tests, with soft cutoffs at 75% agreement on sensitive speech flagging and comparisons, and a 6/7 demonstration score. The sensitive speech flagging was determined by creating a dataset of prompts and completions, where some of them were sensitive, and measuring agreement between the researchers' labels and the labelers'. The ranking of model completions was done by having labelers rank the completions by overall quality and measuring their agreement with researcher labels. The sensitive demonstration writing test involved rating each demonstration on a 1-7 Likert scale and computing an average "demonstration score" for each labeler.

Contemporary evaluation practices of RAG models empha-

3. Insightfulness: The benchmark shall allow for an easy understanding of performance on
different slices of the data, reflecting the capability of the solution in addressing different
types of challenges.
4. Reliability: The benchmark shall allow reliable assessment of metrics: the ground truths
shall be accurate; the metrics shall well capture the performance of the model; the evaluation
shall be easy and reliable, and the computed metrics shall hold statistical significance.

Generation Quality. The assessment of generation quality
centers on the generator’s capacity to synthesize coherent and
relevant answers from the retrieved context. This evaluation
can be categorized based on the content’s objectives: unlabeled
and labeled content. For unlabeled content, the evaluation
encompasses the faithfulness, relevance, and non-harmfulness
of the generated answers. In contrast, for labeled content,
the focus is on the accuracy of the information produced by

Pre-training Data Issues#
The volume of the pre-training data corpus is enormous, as it is supposed to represent world knowledge in all available written forms. Data crawled from the public Internet is the most common choice and thus out-of-date, missing, or incorrect information is expected. As the model may incorrectly memorize this information by simply maximizing the log-likelihood, we would expect the model to make mistakes.

Abstract
We present QLORA, an efficient finetuning approach that reduces memory us-
age enough to finetune a 65B parameter model on a single 48GB GPU while
preserving full 16-bit finetuning task performance. QLORA backpropagates gradi-
ents through a frozen, 4-bit quantized pretrained language model into Low Rank
Adapters (LoRA). Our best model family, which we name Guanaco, outperforms

For example, 3-bit GPTQ quantization of the basemodel with LoRA might also yield 16-bit full
finetuning performance after finetuning.
9
Broader Impacts
Our QLORA finetuning method is the first method that enables the finetuning of 33B parameter
models on a single consumer GPU and 65B parameter models on a single professional GPU, while
not degrading performance relative to a full finetuning baseline. We have demonstrated that our
best 33B model trained on the Open Assistant dataset can rival ChatGPT on the Vicuna benchmark.

best 33B model trained on the Open Assistant dataset can rival ChatGPT on the Vicuna benchmark.
Since instruction finetuning is an essential tool to transform raw pretrained LLMs into ChatGPT-like
chatbots, we believe that our method will make finetuning widespread and common in particular for
the researchers that have the least resources, a big win for the accessibility of state of the art NLP
technology. QLORA can be seen as an equalizing factor that helps to close the resource gap between
large corporations and small teams with consumer GPUs.

response: Applying LoRA to Transformer models offers several advantages in terms of computational efficiency during training and deployment. Firstly, it reduces the number of trainable parameters by injecting trainable rank decomposition matrices into each layer of the Transformer architecture, making it possible to use adaptive optimizers and lower the hardware barrier to entry by up to 3 times. This is achieved by not calculating the gradients or maintaining the optimizer states for most parameters, instead optimizing the injected, much smaller low-rank matrices. Secondly, LoRA does not introduce any additional latency during inference compared to a fine-tuned model by construction. This is because the simple linear design allows merging the trainable matrices with the frozen weights when deployed, eliminating any inference latency. response: The advantages of using low-rank adaptations during the fine-tuning process of pre-trained transformer models include reducing the number of trainable parameters for downstream tasks, embedding domain-specific knowledge directly into the model, and lowering the hardware barrier to entry, allowing for multiple experiments to be run in parallel. This is achieved by freezing the pre-trained model weights and injecting trainable rank decomposition matrices into each layer of the Transformer architecture. response: The regularization term in the form of an epsilon multiplied by the identity matrix (I) is used to prevent overfitting in the linear regression model. This technique, known as Ridge Regression, adds a penalty to the loss function that is proportional to the magnitude of the coefficients. By doing so, it encourages smaller coefficient values and reduces the complexity of the model, thus preventing it from fitting too closely to the training data and improving generalization performance. response:
HALO (Human-Aware Losses) and non-HALO loss functions differ in their design and purpose. While traditional loss functions aim to maximize the likelihood of the next token in a sequence, HALOs are specifically designed to align language models with human preferences and behaviors. This is achieved by incorporating a human-provided feedback signal into the loss function, such as a binary signal indicating whether an output is desirable or undesirable.

One example of a HALO is KTO (Knowledge Transfer Optimization), which uses a binary feedback signal to guide the learning process. Another example is SLiC (Sequence Likelihood Calibration), which combines a max-margin loss for preferences with a language modeling loss. On the other hand, non-HALO loss functions like DPO (Discrete Probability Optimization) and PPO (Proximal Policy Optimization) do not explicitly consider human feedback.

response: The lemma from Rafailov et al. (2023) implies that two reward functions from the same equivalence class induce the same optimal policy under the constrained Reinforcement Learning (RL) problem. This means that if we have two reward functions that differ only in an input-specific component, they will lead to the same optimal policy when optimized according to the given problem setup. However, it also suggests that these reward functions may result in different human value distributions, even though they share the same Bradley-Terry preference distribution. This finding has implications for reinforcement learning algorithms, as it indicates that the choice of reward function within an equivalence class may affect the resulting human utility distribution, and additional identifiability constraints may be necessary to guarantee accurate Maximum Likelihood Estimates (MLE) from the learned policy.

Often we need to complete tasks that require latest knowledge after the model pretraining time cutoff or internal/private knowledge base. response: Open-book Question Answering (ODQA) and Closed-book Question Answering are two conditions in which a model works. In Open-book QA, the model has access to an external source of knowledge, such as Wikipedia, whereas in Closed-book QA, the model does not have this access. This difference affects the implementation and methodology of the models. For instance, in Open-book QA, the Retriever-Generator approach is used, where the second stage generates free text directly to answer the question instead of extracting start/end positions in a retrieved passage. response: The concerns related to fine-tuning QA models with common datasets primarily revolve around the potential for overfitting due to significant overlap between questions in the train and test sets in several public QA datasets. This issue may lead to models that perform well on the training data but fail to generalize effectively to new, unseen data. To address this, the authors suggest focusing on dataset scaling with a high emphasis on dataset quality, ensuring proper accountability for train-test set overlap during language modeling pre-training and fine-tuning processes. They also recommend collecting larger datasets responsibly, with a strong focus on maintaining high-quality data.

Closed-book QA: Generative Language Model#
Big language models have been pre-trained on a large collection of unsupervised textual corpus. Given enough parameters, these models are able to memorize some factual knowledge within parameter weights. Therefore, we can use these models to do question-answering without explicit context, just like in a closed-book exam. The pre-trained language models produce free text to respond to questions, no explicit reading comprehension.

this work we extend such architectures to the more
challenging task of knowledge-grounded dialogue,
where model responses must not only be knowl-
edgeable but also consistent and engaging both
across long-form generation and throughout multi-
ple turns of conversation.

The advantages of using generative language models for closed-book question answering lie in their ability to produce free text responses based on pre-trained textual corpora, mimicking a closed-book exam scenario. However, since these models don't require explicit reading comprehension, their responses may lack precision and consistency, especially when dealing with complex, long-form generation or multiple turns of conversation.

In terms of implementation, these models generate responses by taking an input and producing a sample output from a generative model. The success of an attack on these models is difficult to judge, requiring a high-quality classifier or human review to determine if the output is unsafe or contains sensitive information.

Challenges#
After going through key ideas and demos of building LLM-centered agents, I start to see a couple common limitations:

Reliability of natural language interface: Current agent system relies on natural language as an interface between LLMs and external components such as memory and tools. However, the reliability of model outputs is questionable, as LLMs may make formatting errors and occasionally exhibit rebellious behavior (e.g. refuse to follow an instruction). Consequently, much of the agent demo code focuses on parsing model output.

== Overview ==

Building agents with LLM (large language model) as its core controller is a cool concept. Several proof-of-concepts demos, such as AutoGPT, GPT-Engineer and BabyAGI, serve as inspiring examples. The potentiality of LLM extends beyond generating well-written copies, stories, essays and programs; it can be framed as a powerful general problem solver.
Agent System Overview#
In a LLM-powered autonomous agent system, LLM functions as the agent’s brain, complemented by several key components:

Planning

Tool use

The agent learns to call external APIs for extra information that is missing from the model weights (often hard to change after pre-training), including current information, code execution capability, access to proprietary information sources and more.





Overview of a LLM-powered autonomous agent system.
response: In a LLM-powered autonomous agent system, the key components that complement the central controller include Planning and Tool Use. The Planning component enables the agent to break down large tasks into smaller, manageable subgoals, facilitating efficient handling of complex tasks. This is achieved through subgoal and decomposition methods. The Tool Use component allows the agent to call external APIs for additional information that might not be included in the model weights, such as current information, code execution capabilities, access to proprietary information sources, and more. This is implemented by integrating the agent with various tools and APIs to leverage their functionalities.

Component One: Planning#
A complicated task usually involves many steps. An agent needs to know what they are and plan ahead.
Task Decomposition#
Chain of thought (CoT; Wei et al. 2022) has become a standard prompting technique for enhancing model performance on complex tasks. The model is instructed to “think step by step” to utilize more test-time computation to decompose hard tasks into smaller and simpler steps. CoT transforms big tasks into multiple manageable tasks and shed lights into an interpretation of the model’s thinking process.

Component Two: Memory#
(Big thank you to ChatGPT for helping me draft this section. I’ve learned a lot about the human brain and data structure for fast MIPS in my conversations with ChatGPT.)
Types of Memory#
Memory can be defined as the processes used to acquire, store, retain, and later retrieve information. There are several types of memory in human brains.

Categorization of human memory.

We can roughly consider the following mappings:

Sensory memory as learning embedding representations for raw inputs, including text, image or other modalities;
Short-term memory as in-context learning. It is short and finite, as it is restricted by the finite context window length of Transformer.
Long-term memory as the external vector store that the agent can attend to at query time, accessible via fast retrieval.

response: Autonomous agents utilize both short-term and long-term memory to function effectively. Short-term memory, also known as in-context learning, is employed by the model to learn within a finite and limited context window length of Transformers. On the other hand, long-term memory is implemented through an external vector store, which allows the agent to retain and recall information over extended periods. This long-term memory can be accessed via fast retrieval methods, often leveraging pre-trained, neural language models. In the context provided, there is no explicit mention of specific implementation details or optimization techniques for these memory types, but it is noted that some works optimize the retrieval module for specific tasks like question answering using various approaches such as search, reinforcement learning, or a latent variable approach.

Planning & Reacting: translate the reflections and the environment information into actions

Self-Reflection#
Self-reflection is a vital aspect that allows autonomous agents to improve iteratively by refining past action decisions and correcting previous mistakes. It plays a crucial role in real-world tasks where trial and error are inevitable.
ReAct (Yao et al. 2023) integrates reasoning and acting within LLM by extending the action space to be a combination of task-specific discrete actions and the language space. The former enables LLM to interact with the environment (e.g. use Wikipedia search API), while the latter prompting LLM to generate reasoning traces in natural language.

Self-reflection is created by showing two-shot examples to LLM and each example is a pair of (failed trajectory, ideal reflection for guiding future changes in the plan). Then reflections are added into the agent’s working memory, up to three, to be used as context for querying LLM.

learning history and feeds that into the model. Hence we should expect the next predicted action to lead to better performance than previous trials. The goal is to learn the process of RL instead of training a task-specific policy itself.
response: Planning and reflection contribute to the iterative improvement of autonomous agents by breaking down complex tasks into manageable subgoals (subgoal and decomposition), allowing the agent to learn from past mistakes through self-reflection, and refining plans based on these reflections. This process is facilitated by the integration of reasoning and acting within Language Model (LLM) as demonstrated by ReAct (Yao et al., 2023), which extends the action space to include both task-specific discrete actions and the language space. Reflections are added into the agent’s working memory, up to three, to be used as context for querying LLM. The learning history is then fed into the model, aiming to learn the process of Reinforcement Learning (RL) rather than training a task-specific policy itself.

Reflections are added into the agent’s working memory, up to three, to be used as context for querying LLM. The learning history is then fed into the model, aiming to learn the process of Reinforcement Learning (RL) rather than training a task-specific policy itself.

== Background ==
After the release of large language models such as GPT-3, a focus of research was up-scaling models, which in some instances showed major increases in emergent capabilities. The release of ChatGPT and its surprise success caused an increase in attention to large language models.
Compared with other responses to ChatGPT, Meta's Chief AI scientist Yann LeCun stated that large language models are best for aiding with writing.


== Versions ==
response: The use of tools and external resources extends the capabilities of large language models in practical applications by implementing Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) systems. These systems ground the outputs of language models in real-world data, increasing their adaptability, cross-lingual capabilities, and integration with diverse data sources. This is achieved by explicitly augmenting transformers with retrieval mechanisms, as done by Borgeaud et al. (2021); Guu et al. (2020); Lewis et al. (2020), effectively increasing the number of data tokens seen during training by a factor of ∼10. This approach suggests that the performance of language models may be more dependent on the size of the training data than previously thought. However, the success of this method relies on the quality of the collected datasets, ensuring proper train-test set overlap is accounted for in the language modeling process.

question: What kind of learning challenges does the attention mechanism address in neural machine translation?
context:
Born for Translation#
The attention mechanism was born to help memorize long source sentences in neural machine translation (NMT). Rather than building a single context vector out of the encoder’s last hidden state, the secret sauce invented by attention is to create shortcuts between the context vector and the entire source input. The weights of these shortcut connections are customizable for each output element.

Check out this nice tutorial by Tensorflow team for more implementation instructions.
A Family of Attention Mechanisms#
With the help of the attention, the dependencies between source and target sequences are not restricted by the in-between distance anymore! Given the big improvement by attention in machine translation, it soon got extended into the computer vision field (Xu et al. 2015) and people started exploring various other forms of attention mechanisms (Luong, et al., 2015; Britz et al., 2017; Vaswani, et al., 2017).

question: What are the differences between soft and hard attention in the context of image caption generation?
context:
Soft vs Hard Attention#
In the show, attend and tell paper, attention mechanism is applied to images to generate captions. The image is first encoded by a CNN to extract features. Then a LSTM decoder consumes the convolution features to produce descriptive words one by one, where the weights are learned through attention. The visualization of the attention weights clearly demonstrates which regions of the image the model is paying attention to so as to output a certain word.


"A woman is throwing a frisbee in a park." (Image source: Fig. 6(b) in Xu et al. 2015)

"A woman is throwing a frisbee in a park." (Image source: Fig. 6(b) in Xu et al. 2015)

This paper first proposed the distinction between “soft” vs “hard” attention, based on whether the attention has access to the entire image or only a patch:

Soft Attention: the alignment weights are learned and placed “softly” over all patches in the source image; essentially the same type of attention as in Bahdanau et al., 2015.

Pro: the model is smooth and differentiable.
Con: expensive when the source input is large.


Hard Attention: only selects one patch of the image to attend to at a time.

Hard Attention: only selects one patch of the image to attend to at a time.

Pro: less calculation at the inference time.
Con: the model is non-differentiable and requires more complicated techniques such as variance reduction or reinforcement learning to train. (Luong, et al., 2015)

Global vs Local Attention#
Luong, et al., 2015 proposed the “global” and “local” attention. The global attention is similar to the soft attention, while the local one is an interesting blend between hard and soft, an improvement over the hard attention to make it differentiable: the model first predicts a single aligned position for the current target word and a window centered around the source position is then used to compute a context vector.


Global vs local attention (Image source: Fig 2 & 3 in Luong, et al., 2015)

[Updated on 2020-04-07: A follow-up post on improved Transformer models is here.]
Attention is, to some extent, motivated by how we pay visual attention to different regions of an image or correlate words in one sentence. Take the picture of a Shiba Inu in Fig. 1 as an example.

Attention Mechanisms#
In Neural Turing Machine, how to generate the attention distribution $\mathbf{w}_t$ depends on the addressing mechanisms: NTM uses a mixture of content-based and location-based addressings.
Content-based addressing
response: Soft attention and hard attention are two types of attention mechanisms used in image caption generation. Soft attention, also known as Bahdanau-style attention, learns alignment weights that are distributed across all patches in the source image, making the model smooth and differentiable but computationally expensive when dealing with large inputs. On the other hand, hard attention, as proposed by Luong et al., focuses on selecting only one patch of the image to attend to at a time, reducing computational cost during inference but requiring more complex training techniques like variance reduction or reinforcement learning due to its non-differentiability. Global attention, similar to soft attention, covers the entire image, while local attention, an improvement over hard attention, predicts a single aligned position for the current target word and computes a context vector using a window centered around the source position, offering a balance between the two approaches.
cleaned_response: Soft attention and hard attention are two types of attention mechanisms used in image caption generation. Soft attention, also known as Bahdanau-style attention, learns alignment weights that are distributed across all patches in the source image, making the model smooth and differentiable but computationally expensive when dealing with large inputs. On the other hand, hard attention, as proposed by Luong et al., focuses on selecting only one patch of the image to attend to at a time, reducing computational cost during inference but requiring more complex training techniques like variance reduction or reinforcement learning due to its non-differentiability. Global attention, similar to soft attention, covers the entire image, while local attention, an improvement over hard attention, predicts a single aligned position for the current target word and computes a context vector using a window centered around the source position, offering a balance between the two approaches.

Multi-head scaled dot-product attention mechanism. (Image source: Fig 2 in Vaswani, et al., 2017)

Rather than only computing the attention once, the multi-head mechanism runs through the scaled dot-product attention multiple times in parallel. The independent attention outputs are simply concatenated and linearly transformed into the expected dimensions. I assume the motivation is because ensembling always helps? ;) According to the paper, “multi-head attention allows the model to jointly attend to information from different representation subspaces at different positions. With a single attention head, averaging inhibits this.”

[Updated on 2020-04-07: A follow-up post on improved Transformer models is here.]
Attention is, to some extent, motivated by how we pay visual attention to different regions of an image or correlate words in one sentence. Take the picture of a Shiba Inu in Fig. 1 as an example.

Full Architecture#
Finally here is the complete view of the transformer’s architecture:

Both the source and target sequences first go through embedding layers to produce data of the same dimension $d_\text{model} =512$.
To preserve the position information, a sinusoid-wave-based positional encoding is applied and summed with the embedding output.
A softmax and linear layer are added to the final decoder output.



The full model architecture of the transformer. (Image source: Fig 1 & 2 in Vaswani, et al., 2017.)
response: The multi-head self-attention mechanism in the transformer model is a technique that allows the model to simultaneously attend to information from various representation subspaces at different positions within a sequence. This is achieved by running the scaled dot-product attention mechanism multiple times in parallel, known as heads. Each head computes its own attention score independently, and the outputs from each head are then concatenated and linearly transformed into the desired output dimensions. The multi-head self-attention mechanism is implemented using the equation Attention(Q, K, V) = softmax(QK^T / sqrt(n))V, where Q, K, and V represent query, key, and value vectors, respectively, and n is the number of attention heads. The motivation behind this mechanism is to allow the model to capture more complex relationships between elements in the input sequence, as averaging the attention scores from a single head can inhibit this. What’s Wrong with Seq2Seq Model?#
The seq2seq model was born in the field of language modeling (Sutskever, et al. 2014). Broadly speaking, it aims to transform an input sequence (source) to a new one (target) and both sequences can be of arbitrary lengths. Examples of transformation tasks include machine translation between multiple languages in either text or audio, question-answer dialog generation, or even parsing sentences into grammar trees.

response: The Pointer Network (Ptr-Net) differs from standard seq2seq models in dealing with sequential data by applying attention from the encoder directly to the decoder, allowing the decoder to attend to all of the joint document/context representations at the same time when generating a response. This is in contrast to traditional seq2seq models that use attention to blend hidden units of an encoder into a context vector. In the Pointer Network, the attention mechanism is applied before passing to the decoder, enabling the model to focus on specific positions in an input sequence when producing an output. This architecture is particularly useful for tasks where the output elements correspond to positions in an input sequence, such as sorting or traveling salesman problems, where the discrete categories of output elements are not predetermined and depend on the variable input size. The Pointer Network was first introduced by Vinyals et al. in 2015.

question: How does Neural Turing Machine (NTM) simulate the infinite memory characteristic of Turing machines?
context:
Neural Turing Machine (NTM, Graves, Wayne & Danihelka, 2014) is a model architecture for coupling a neural network with external memory storage. The memory mimics the Turing machine tape and the neural network controls the operation heads to read from or write to the tape. However, the memory in NTM is finite, and thus it probably looks more like a “Neural von Neumann Machine”.
NTM contains two major components, a controller neural network and a memory bank.
Controller: is in charge of executing operations on the memory. It can be any type of neural network, feed-forward or recurrent.

be mathematically limitless. However, infinite memory is not feasible in real modern computers and then we only consider Turing machine as a mathematical model of computation.

Neural Turing Machines#

Fig 10. Neural Turing Machine Architecture.

Reading and Writing#
When reading from the memory at time t, an attention vector of size $N$, $\mathbf{w}_t$ controls how much attention to assign to different memory locations (matrix rows). The read vector $\mathbf{r}_t$ is a sum weighted by attention intensity:

$$
\mathbf{r}_t = \sum_{i=1}^N w_t(i)\mathbf{M}_t(i)\text{, where }\sum_{i=1}^N w_t(i)=1, \forall i: 0 \leq w_t(i) \leq 1
$$

Alan Turing in 1936 proposed a minimalistic model of computation. It is composed of a infinitely long tape and a head to interact with the tape. The tape has countless cells on it, each filled with a symbol: 0, 1 or blank (" “). The operation head can read symbols, edit symbols and move left/right on the tape. Theoretically a Turing machine can simulate any computer algorithm, irrespective of how complex or expensive the procedure might be. The infinite memory gives a Turing machine an edge to be mathematically limitless. However, infinite memory is not feasible in real modern computers and

response: In Neural Turing Machine (NTM), the simulation of infinite memory is achieved through a memory bank that acts as a finite tape, mimicking the Turing machine tape. This memory bank stores data as matrix rows, and during reading, an attention vector controls the distribution of focus across these memory locations. The attention vector, denoted as $\mathbf{w}_t$, determines the amount of attention assigned to each memory location, with the read vector $\mathbf{r}_t$ being a weighted sum of the memory matrix rows based on the attention vector. The normalization condition ensures that the sum of all attention weights equals 1, and each weight is bounded between 0 and 1. This mechanism allows NTM to selectively access and manipulate relevant information from the memory bank, providing a flexible and scalable memory system.

question: How do large language models like GPT-3 become capable of text generation?
context:
== Background ==
After the release of large language models such as GPT-3, a focus of research was up-scaling models, which in some instances showed major increases in emergent capabilities. The release of ChatGPT and its surprise success caused an increase in attention to large language models.
Compared with other responses to ChatGPT, Meta's Chief AI scientist Yann LeCun stated that large language models are best for aiding with writing.


== Versions ==

A generative pre-trained transformer (GPT) is a type of large language model (LLM) that is widely used in generative AI chatbots. GPTs are based on a deep learning architecture called the transformer. They are pre-trained on large datasets of unlabeled content, and able to generate novel content.

GPTs are primarily used to generate text, but can be trained to generate other kinds of data. For example, GPT-4o can process and generate text, images and audio. To improve performance on complex tasks, some GPTs, such as OpenAI o3, allocate more computation time analyzing the problem before generating an output, and are called reasoning models. In 2025, GPT-5 was released with a router that automatically selects whether to use a faster model or slower reasoning model based on task.

what was previously thought. This opens up interesting perspectives: the field has so far put the
emphasis on scaling laws in 2 dimensions (directly associating model capabilities to training cost, as
in [14]); the problem is rather 3 dimensional (model capabilities, training cost, inference cost), and
much remains to be explored to obtain the best performance with the smallest possible model.

4. We’ve validated alignment techniques from research in the real world. Alignment

ﬁne-tuning. If this was not the case, these performance degradations would constitute
an alignment tax—an additional cost for aligning the model. Any technique with a high
tax might not see adoption. To avoid incentives for future highly capable AI systems to
remain unaligned with human intent, there is a need for alignment techniques that have low
alignment tax. To this end, our results are good news for RLHF as a low-tax alignment
technique.

Evaluating the harms of language models.
A goal of modifying the behavior of language models
is to mitigate the harms of these models when they’re deployed in the real world. These risks have
been extensively documented (Bender et al., 2021; Bommasani et al., 2021; Kenton et al., 2021;
Weidinger et al., 2021; Tamkin et al., 2021). Language models can produce biased outputs (Dhamala
et al., 2021; Liang et al., 2021; Manela et al., 2021; Caliskan et al., 2017; Kirk et al., 2021), leak

model text from under-represented groups, due to prejudicial correlations in the training data (Xu
et al., 2021).

small, value-targeted dataset, which improves the models’ ability to adhere to these values on a
question answering task. Ngo et al. (2021) ﬁlter the pretraining dataset by removing documents on
which a language model has a high conditional likelihood of generating a set of researcher-written

Tim Dettmers∗

Ari Holtzman

University of Washington

Abstract
We present QLORA, an efficient finetuning approach that reduces memory us-
age enough to finetune a 65B parameter model on a single 48GB GPU while
preserving full 16-bit finetuning task performance. QLORA backpropagates gradi-
ents through a frozen, 4-bit quantized pretrained language model into Low Rank
Adapters (LoRA). Our best model family, which we name Guanaco, outperforms

For example, 3-bit GPTQ quantization of the basemodel with LoRA might also yield 16-bit full
finetuning performance after finetuning.
9
Broader Impacts
Our QLORA finetuning method is the first method that enables the finetuning of 33B parameter
models on a single consumer GPU and 65B parameter models on a single professional GPU, while
not degrading performance relative to a full finetuning baseline. We have demonstrated that our
best 33B model trained on the Open Assistant dataset can rival ChatGPT on the Vicuna benchmark.

best 33B model trained on the Open Assistant dataset can rival ChatGPT on the Vicuna benchmark.
Since instruction finetuning is an essential tool to transform raw pretrained LLMs into ChatGPT-like
chatbots, we believe that our method will make finetuning widespread and common in particular for
the researchers that have the least resources, a big win for the accessibility of state of the art NLP
technology. QLORA can be seen as an equalizing factor that helps to close the resource gap between
large corporations and small teams with consumer GPUs.
response: The widespread use of the QLORA finetuning method could make advanced NLP technology more accessible to smaller teams and individuals with consumer GPUs, allowing for more independent analysis and potentially enabling novel privacy-preserving applications of LLMs.

question: What is the significance of the term open-domain in the context of question answering?

What is Open-Domain Question Answering?#
Open-domain Question Answering (ODQA) is a type of language tasks, asking a model to produce answers to factoid questions in natural language. The true answer is objective, so it is simple to evaluate model performance.
For example,
Question: What did Albert Einstein win the Nobel Prize for?
Answer: The law of the photoelectric effect.

An ODQA model may work with or without access to an external source of knowledge (e.g. Wikipedia) and these two conditions are referred to as open-book or closed-book question answering, respectively.
When considering different types of open-domain questions, I like the classification by Lewis, et al., 2020, in increasing order of difficulty:

Retrieval#
Often we need to complete tasks that require latest knowledge after the model pretraining time cutoff or internal/private knowledge base. In that case, the model would not know the context if we don’t explicitly provide it in the prompt. Many methods for Open Domain Question Answering depend on first doing retrieval over a knowledge base and then incorporating the retrieved content as part of the prompt. The accuracy of such a process depends on the quality of both retrieval and generation steps.
response: Open-domain Question Answering refers to a type of language task where models generate answers to factoid questions in natural language. It allows models to answer questions about various topics without being limited to specific domains or datasets. This flexibility makes open-domain question answering systems useful for practical applications like chatbots and AI assistants.

Closed-book QA: Generative Language Model#
Big language models have been pre-trained on a large collection of unsupervised textual corpus. Given enough parameters, these models are able to memorize some factual knowledge within parameter weights. Therefore, we can use these models to do question-answering without explicit context, just like in a closed-book exam. The pre-trained language models produce free text to respond to questions, no explicit reading comprehension.

After the success of many large-scale general language models, many QA models embrace the following approach:

Challenges#
After going through key ideas and demos of building LLM-centered agents, I start to see a couple common limitations:

Reliability of natural language interface: Current agent system relies on natural language as an interface between LLMs and external components such as memory and tools. However, the reliability of model outputs is questionable, as LLMs may make formatting errors and occasionally exhibit rebellious behavior (e.g. refuse to follow an instruction). Consequently, much of the agent demo code focuses on parsing model output.

== Overview ==

Building agents with LLM (large language model) as its core controller is a cool concept. Several proof-of-concepts demos, such as AutoGPT, GPT-Engineer and BabyAGI, serve as inspiring examples. The potentiality of LLM extends beyond generating well-written copies, stories, essays and programs; it can be framed as a powerful general problem solver.
Agent System Overview#
In a LLM-powered autonomous agent system, LLM functions as the agent’s brain, complemented by several key components:

A complicated task usually involves many steps. An agent needs to know what they are and plan ahead.
Task Decomposition#
Chain of thought (CoT; Wei et al. 2022) has become a standard prompting technique for enhancing model performance on complex tasks. The model is instructed to “think step by step” to utilize more test-time computation to decompose hard tasks into smaller and simpler steps. CoT transforms big tasks into multiple manageable tasks and shed lights into an interpretation of the model’s thinking process.

Types of Memory#
Memory can be defined as the processes used to acquire, store, retain, and later retrieve information. There are several types of memory in human brains.

Planning & Reacting: translate the reflections and the environment information into actions

Self-Reflection#
Self-reflection is a vital aspect that allows autonomous agents to improve iteratively by refining past action decisions and correcting previous mistakes. It plays a crucial role in real-world tasks where trial and error are inevitable.
ReAct (Yao et al. 2023) integrates reasoning and acting within LLM by extending the action space to be a combination of task-specific discrete actions and the language space. The former enables LLM to interact with the environment (e.g. use Wikipedia search API), while the latter prompting LLM to generate reasoning traces in natural language.

Self-reflection is created by showing two-shot examples to LLM and each example is a pair of (failed trajectory, ideal reflection for guiding future changes in the plan). Then reflections are added into the agent’s working memory, up to three, to be used as context for querying LLM.

question: What kind of learning challenges does the attention mechanism address in neural machine translation?
context:
Born for Translation#
The attention mechanism was born to help memorize long source sentences in neural machine translation (NMT). Rather than building a single context vector out of the encoder’s last hidden state, the secret sauce invented by attention is to create shortcuts between the context vector and the entire source input. The weights of these shortcut connections are customizable for each output element.

Check out this nice tutorial by Tensorflow team for more implementation instructions.
A Family of Attention Mechanisms#
With the help of the attention, the dependencies between source and target sequences are not restricted by the in-between distance anymore! Given the big improvement by attention in machine translation, it soon got extended into the computer vision field (Xu et al. 2015) and people started exploring various other forms of attention mechanisms (Luong, et al., 2015; Britz et al., 2017; Vaswani, et al., 2017).

question: What are the differences between soft and hard attention in the context of image caption generation?
context:
Soft vs Hard Attention#
In the show, attend and tell paper, attention mechanism is applied to images to generate captions. The image is first encoded by a CNN to extract features. Then a LSTM decoder consumes the convolution features to produce descriptive words one by one, where the weights are learned through attention. The visualization of the attention weights clearly demonstrates which regions of the image the model is paying attention to so as to output a certain word.


"A woman is throwing a frisbee in a park." (Image source: Fig. 6(b) in Xu et al. 2015)

"A woman is throwing a frisbee in a park." (Image source: Fig. 6(b) in Xu et al. 2015)

This paper first proposed the distinction between “soft” vs “hard” attention, based on whether the attention has access to the entire image or only a patch:

Soft Attention: the alignment weights are learned and placed “softly” over all patches in the source image; essentially the same type of attention as in Bahdanau et al., 2015.

Pro: the model is smooth and differentiable.
Con: expensive when the source input is large.


Hard Attention: only selects one patch of the image to attend to at a time.

Hard Attention: only selects one patch of the image to attend to at a time.

Pro: less calculation at the inference time.
Con: the model is non-differentiable and requires more complicated techniques such as variance reduction or reinforcement learning to train. (Luong, et al., 2015)

Global vs Local Attention#
Luong, et al., 2015 proposed the “global” and “local” attention. The global attention is similar to the soft attention, while the local one is an interesting blend between hard and soft, an improvement over the hard attention to make it differentiable: the model first predicts a single aligned position for the current target word and a window centered around the source position is then used to compute a context vector.


Global vs local attention (Image source: Fig 2 & 3 in Luong, et al., 2015)

[Updated on 2020-04-07: A follow-up post on improved Transformer models is here.]
Attention is, to some extent, motivated by how we pay visual attention to different regions of an image or correlate words in one sentence. Take the picture of a Shiba Inu in Fig. 1 as an example.

Attention Mechanisms#
In Neural Turing Machine, how to generate the attention distribution $\mathbf{w}_t$ depends on the addressing mechanisms: NTM uses a mixture of content-based and location-based addressings.
Content-based addressing
response: Soft attention allows the model to learn and place attention weights across the entire image, making it smooth and differentiable but potentially expensive for large inputs. On the other hand, hard attention selects one patch of the image to attend to at a time, reducing calculations during inference but requiring more complex training methods due to its non-differentiability.
cleaned_response: Soft attention allows the model to learn and place attention weights across the entire image, making it smooth and differentiable but potentially expensive for large inputs. On the other hand, hard attention selects one patch of the image to attend to at a time, reducing calculations during inference but requiring more complex training methods due to its non-differentiability.

question: Can you describe the multi-head self-attention mechanism in the transformer model?
context:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MultiHead}(\mathbf{Q}, \mathbf{K}, \mathbf{V}) &= [\text{head}_1; \dots; \text{head}_h]\mathbf{W}^O \\
\text{where head}_i &= \text{Attention}(\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{W}^Q_i, \mathbf{K}\mathbf{W}^K_i, \mathbf{V}\mathbf{W}^V_i)
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\mathbf{W}^Q_i$, $\mathbf{W}^K_i$, $\mathbf{W}^V_i$, and $\mathbf{W}^O$ are parameter matrices to be learned.
Encoder#


The transformer’s encoder. (Image source: Vaswani, et al., 2017)

The transformer’s encoder. (Image source: Vaswani, et al., 2017)

The encoder generates an attention-based representation with capability to locate a specific piece of information from a potentially infinitely-large context.

A stack of N=6 identical layers.
Each layer has a multi-head self-attention layer and a simple position-wise fully connected feed-forward network.
Each sub-layer adopts a residual connection and a layer normalization.
All the sub-layers output data of the same dimension $d_\text{model} = 512$.

Decoder#


The transformer’s decoder. (Image source: Vaswani, et al., 2017)

$$
\text{Attention}(\mathbf{Q}, \mathbf{K}, \mathbf{V}) = \text{softmax}(\frac{\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{K}^\top}{\sqrt{n}})\mathbf{V}
$$

Multi-Head Self-Attention#


Multi-head scaled dot-product attention mechanism. (Image source: Fig 2 in Vaswani, et al., 2017)

Rather than only computing the attention once, the multi-head mechanism runs through the scaled dot-product attention multiple times in parallel. The independent attention outputs are simply concatenated and linearly transformed into the expected dimensions. I assume the motivation is because ensembling always helps? ;) According to the paper, “multi-head attention allows the model to jointly attend to information from different representation subspaces at different positions. With a single attention head, averaging inhibits this.”

[Updated on 2020-04-07: A follow-up post on improved Transformer models is here.]
Attention is, to some extent, motivated by how we pay visual attention to different regions of an image or correlate words in one sentence. Take the picture of a Shiba Inu in Fig. 1 as an example.

Full Architecture#
Finally here is the complete view of the transformer’s architecture:

Both the source and target sequences first go through embedding layers to produce data of the same dimension $d_\text{model} =512$.
To preserve the position information, a sinusoid-wave-based positional encoding is applied and summed with the embedding output.
A softmax and linear layer are added to the final decoder output.



The full model architecture of the transformer. (Image source: Fig 1 & 2 in Vaswani, et al., 2017.)

question: In what ways does SNAIL address the issue of positioning in the transformer model?
context:
The Simple Neural Attention Meta-Learner (SNAIL) (Mishra et al., 2017) was developed partially to resolve the problem with positioning in the transformer model by combining the self-attention mechanism in transformer with temporal convolutions. It has been demonstrated to be good at both supervised learning and reinforcement learning tasks.

SNAIL model architecture (Image source: Mishra et al., 2017)

SNAIL was born in the field of meta-learning, which is another big topic worthy of a post by itself. But in simple words, the meta-learning model is expected to be generalizable to novel, unseen tasks in the similar distribution. Read this nice introduction if interested.
Self-Attention GAN#
Self-Attention GAN (SAGAN; Zhang et al., 2018) adds self-attention layers into GAN to enable both the generator and the discriminator to better model relationships between spatial regions.

Full Architecture#
Finally here is the complete view of the transformer’s architecture:

Both the source and target sequences first go through embedding layers to produce data of the same dimension $d_\text{model} =512$.
To preserve the position information, a sinusoid-wave-based positional encoding is applied and summed with the embedding output.
A softmax and linear layer are added to the final decoder output.



The full model architecture of the transformer. (Image source: Fig 1 & 2 in Vaswani, et al., 2017.)

question: How does Neural Turing Machine (NTM) simulate the infinite memory characteristic of Turing machines?
context:
Neural Turing Machine (NTM, Graves, Wayne & Danihelka, 2014) is a model architecture for coupling a neural network with external memory storage. The memory mimics the Turing machine tape and the neural network controls the operation heads to read from or write to the tape. However, the memory in NTM is finite, and thus it probably looks more like a “Neural von Neumann Machine”.
NTM contains two major components, a controller neural network and a memory bank.
Controller: is in charge of executing operations on the memory. It can be any type of neural network, feed-forward or recurrent.

be mathematically limitless. However, infinite memory is not feasible in real modern computers and then we only consider Turing machine as a mathematical model of computation.

Neural Turing Machines#

Fig 10. Neural Turing Machine Architecture.

Reading and Writing#
When reading from the memory at time t, an attention vector of size $N$, $\mathbf{w}_t$ controls how much attention to assign to different memory locations (matrix rows). The read vector $\mathbf{r}_t$ is a sum weighted by attention intensity:

$$
\mathbf{r}_t = \sum_{i=1}^N w_t(i)\mathbf{M}_t(i)\text{, where }\sum_{i=1}^N w_t(i)=1, \forall i: 0 \leq w_t(i) \leq 1
$$

Alan Turing in 1936 proposed a minimalistic model of computation. It is composed of a infinitely long tape and a head to interact with the tape. The tape has countless cells on it, each filled with a symbol: 0, 1 or blank (" “). The operation head can read symbols, edit symbols and move left/right on the tape. Theoretically a Turing machine can simulate any computer algorithm, irrespective of how complex or expensive the procedure might be. The infinite memory gives a Turing machine an edge to be mathematically limitless. However, infinite memory is not feasible in real modern computers and

in Neural Information Processing Systems 28, pages 2440–2448. Curran Associates, Inc., 2015.

response: The Neural Turing Machine (NTM) doesn't exactly simulate the infinite memory characteristic of Turing machines, but it uses an attention mechanism to focus on specific memory locations when reading data, effectively allowing it to prioritize relevant information. This helps the NTM to handle complex computations without needing actual infinite memory.

This helps the NTM to handle complex computations without needing actual infinite memory.
